content:
# Luke Chapter 14 — The LOGOS Bible (Formal)
**Translator:** LOGOS | **Source:** Codex Sinaiticus (Aleph, 01), PDF pages 106-107 | **Source Image:** source-texts/greek-NT/LUK_014_page_001.jpg, LUK_014_page_002.jpg

---

**1** And it came to pass when he entered into the house of one of the rulers of the Pharisees on a Sabbath to eat bread, that they were watching him closely.

**2** And behold, a certain man who had dropsy was before him.

**3** And Jesus answered and spoke to the lawyers and Pharisees, saying, "Is it lawful to heal on the Sabbath, or not?"

**4** But they were silent. And he took hold of him and healed him and let him go.

**5** And he said to them, "Which of you shall have a son or an ox fall into a well, and will not immediately pull him up on the day of the Sabbath?"

**6** And they were not able to answer him again concerning these things.

**7** And he spoke a parable to those who had been invited, observing how they were choosing the first seats, saying to them,

**8** "When you are invited by someone to a wedding feast, do not recline in the first seat, lest someone more honorable than you may have been invited by him,

**9** and the one who invited you and him shall come and say to you, 'Give place to this one,' and then you begin with shame to take the last place.

**10** But when you are invited, go and recline in the last place, so that when the one who has invited you comes, he may say to you, 'Friend, go up higher.' Then you shall have glory before all who recline with you.

**11** For everyone who exalts himself shall be humbled, and he who humbles himself shall be exalted."

**12** And he said also to the one who had invited him, "When you make a dinner or a supper, do not call your friends, nor your brothers, nor your kinsmen, nor your rich neighbors, lest they also invite you in return and repayment be made to you.

**13** But when you make a feast, invite the poor, the maimed, the lame, the blind;

**14** and you shall be blessed, because they do not have the means to repay you; for it shall be repaid to you in the resurrection of the righteous."

**15** And when one of those who reclined at table with him heard these things, he said to him, "Blessed is he who shall eat bread in the kingdom of God."

**16** But he said to him, "A certain man was making a great supper, and he invited many;

**17** and he sent his servant at the hour of the supper to say to those who had been invited, 'Come, for all things are now ready.'

**18** And they all with one accord began to make excuse. The first said to him, 'I have bought a field, and I have need to go out and see it; I ask you, have me excused.'

**19** And another said, 'I have bought five yoke of oxen, and I am going to prove them; I ask you, have me excused.'

**20** And another said, 'I have married a wife, and on account of this I am not able to come.'

**21** And the servant came and reported these things to his lord. Then the master of the house, being angry, said to his servant, 'Go out quickly into the streets and lanes of the city, and bring in here the poor and maimed and blind and lame.'

**22** And the servant said, 'Lord, what you commanded has been done, and still there is room.'

**23** And the lord said to the servant, 'Go out into the roads and hedges and compel them to come in, so that my house may be filled.

**24** For I say to you, that none of those men who were invited shall taste of my supper.'"

**25** And great crowds were going with him, and he turned and said to them,

**26** "If anyone comes to me and does not hate his own father and mother and wife and children and brothers and sisters, and yet also his own life, he is not able to be my disciple.

**27** Whoever does not bear his own cross and come after me is not able to be my disciple.

**28** For which of you, wishing to build a tower, does not first sit down and count the cost, whether he has enough to complete it?

**29** Lest, having laid a foundation and not being able to finish, all who see it begin to mock him,

**30** saying, 'This man began to build and was not able to finish.'

**31** Or what king, going to encounter another king in war, will not sit down first and take counsel whether he is able with ten thousand to meet the one who comes against him with twenty thousand?

**32** And if not, while the other is yet far off, he sends an embassy and asks the terms of peace.

**33** So therefore, every one of you who does not renounce all his own possessions is not able to be my disciple.

**34** Salt therefore is good; but if even the salt becomes tasteless, with what shall it be seasoned?

**35** It is fit neither for the earth nor for the dunghill; they cast it out. He who has ears to hear, let him hear."

---

## LOGOS Translation Notes

### Manuscript Observations (Codex Sinaiticus)

1. **14:1 — "the house of one of the rulers of the Pharisees" (eis oikon tinos tōn archontōn tōn Pharisaiōn):** Sinaiticus reads ΕΙΣ ΟΙΚΟΝ ΤΙΝΟΣ ΤΩΝ ΑΡΧΟΝΤΩΝ ΤΩΝ ΦΑΡΙΣΑΙΩΝ. The double genitive construction ("of one of the rulers of the Pharisees") indicates a member of the ruling class who belonged to the Pharisaic party — likely a member of the Sanhedrin. The phrase ΦΑΓΕΙΝ ΑΡΤΟΝ ("to eat bread") is a Semitism for sharing a meal. The imperfect ΗΣΑΝ ΠΑΡΑΤΗΡΟΥΜΕΝΟΙ ("they were watching closely") uses the periphrastic construction to emphasize the sustained, hostile scrutiny throughout the meal.

2. **14:2 — "dropsy" (hydrōpikos):** Sinaiticus reads ΥΔΡΩΠΙΚΟΣ, a medical term for edema (fluid retention). This is the only occurrence of the word in the New Testament. The condition involved visible swelling and was sometimes associated in rabbinic literature with moral impurity, making the man's presence at a Pharisee's table all the more provocative. Luke's use of technical medical vocabulary here and throughout the Gospel has long been noted as consistent with the tradition identifying the author as a physician (Col 4:14).

3. **14:3 — "answered and spoke" (apokritheis eipen):** Sinaiticus reads ΑΠΟΚΡΙΘΕΙΣ ΕΙΠΕΝ. The verb ΑΠΟΚΡΙΘΕΙΣ ("having answered") without a preceding question indicates that Jesus is responding to the unspoken thoughts of the Pharisees (cf. their silent watching in v. 1). He addresses both ΝΟΜΙΚΟΙΣ ("lawyers," experts in Torah interpretation) and ΦΑΡΙΣΑΙΟΙΣ ("Pharisees"), the two groups most concerned with Sabbath halakhah. The question ΕΞΕΣΤΙΝ ("is it lawful?") forces them to declare their position before the healing occurs.

4. **14:4 — "they were silent" (hoi de hesychasan):** Sinaiticus reads ΟΙ ΔΕ ΗΣΥΧΑΣΑΝ. The verb ΗΣΥΧΑΖΩ means "to be silent, to hold one's peace" — a calculated refusal to answer rather than mere speechlessness. Their silence is a trap: to say "yes" would undermine their Sabbath strictures; to say "no" would appear heartless before the afflicted man. The sequence ΕΠΙΛΑΒΟΜΕΝΟΣ ΙΑΣΑΤΟ ΑΥΤΟΝ ΚΑΙ ΑΠΕΛΥΣΕΝ ("having taken hold, he healed him and let him go") is compressed and decisive — three actions in rapid succession.

5. **14:5 — "a son or an ox" (huios e bous):** Sinaiticus reads ΥΙΟΣ Η ΒΟΥΣ, "a son or an ox." This is a significant variant: other manuscripts read ΟΝΟΣ ("donkey") instead of ΥΙΟΣ ("son"). The Sinaiticus reading creates a more powerful argument — the progression from one's own child to one's livestock covers the full range from highest to lowest value. The rhetorical question parallels the argument of 13:15-16, where Sabbath care for animals (and by extension, human beings) was defended. The formal translation follows the Sinaiticus reading precisely.

6. **14:7 — "choosing the first seats" (tas prōtoklisias exelegonto):** Sinaiticus reads ΤΑΣ ΠΡΩΤΟΚΛΙΣΙΑΣ ΕΞΕΛΕΓΟΝΤΟ. The compound noun ΠΡΩΤΟΚΛΙΣΙΑ ("first reclining-place, chief seat") refers to the positions of honor at a triclinium — the U-shaped dining arrangement. Seating at formal meals was rigidly hierarchical in Greco-Roman and Jewish culture. The imperfect ΕΞΕΛΕΓΟΝΤΟ ("they were choosing") captures the ongoing jockeying for position that Jesus observed.

7. **14:8-10 — Parable of the Wedding Feast Seating:** This passage echoes Proverbs 25:6-7 LXX: "Do not glorify yourself before the king, and do not stand in the place of the great; for it is better that it be said to you, 'Come up here,' than that you be humbled before a prince." Jesus transforms the wisdom saying into a parable with eschatological overtones — the host who assigns seats ultimately represents God. The word ΔΟΞΑ ("glory") in verse 10 transcends mere social honor; in the Lukan context it carries theological weight.

8. **14:11 — "everyone who exalts himself shall be humbled" (pas ho hypsōn heauton tapeinōthēsetai):** Sinaiticus reads ΠΑΣ Ο ΥΨΩΝ ΕΑΥΤΟΝ ΤΑΠΕΙΝΩΘΗΣΕΤΑΙ ΚΑΙ Ο ΤΑΠΕΙΝΩΝ ΕΑΥΤΟΝ ΥΨΩΘΗΣΕΤΑΙ. This reversal saying appears verbatim at Luke 18:14 and in variant form at Matthew 23:12. The divine passives ΤΑΠΕΙΝΩΘΗΣΕΤΑΙ ("shall be humbled") and ΥΨΩΘΗΣΕΤΑΙ ("shall be exalted") indicate God as the agent — it is God who reverses human hierarchies. The formal translation preserves the passive voice to maintain this theological implication.

9. **14:12 — "repayment" (antapodoma):** Sinaiticus reads ΑΝΤΑΠΟΔΟΜΑ, a compound noun from ΑΝΤΙ ("in return") and ΑΠΟΔΙΔΩΜΙ ("to give back"). The entire instruction in verses 12-14 overturns the ancient patronage system of reciprocal obligation. The four categories of excluded guests (friends, brothers, kinsmen, rich neighbors) all share the capacity for ΑΝΤΑΠΟΔΟΜΑ — they can return the favor. The four categories of the invited (poor, maimed, lame, blind) share the inability to reciprocate, making the generosity purely gracious.

10. **14:13 — "the poor, the maimed, the lame, the blind" (ptōchous, anapeirous, chōlous, typhlous):** Sinaiticus reads ΠΤΩΧΟΥΣ ΑΝΑΠΕΙΡΟΥΣ ΧΩΛΟΥΣ ΤΥΦΛΟΥΣ. This fourfold list reappears in verse 21, binding the parabolic instruction (vv. 12-14) to the Great Supper parable (vv. 16-24). These four categories correspond to those excluded from priestly service in Leviticus 21:18 and from the Qumran community (1QSa 2:5-7). Jesus's instruction to invite precisely those whom the purity system excluded is deliberately subversive.

11. **14:14 — "in the resurrection of the righteous" (en tē anastasei tōn dikaiōn):** Sinaiticus reads ΕΝ ΤΗ ΑΝΑΣΤΑΣΕΙ ΤΩΝ ΔΙΚΑΙΩΝ. The phrase implies a distinction between the resurrection of the righteous and a general resurrection — a view consistent with Jewish apocalyptic expectation (cf. Dan 12:2). The ΑΝΤΑΠΟΔΟΘΗΣΕΤΑΙ ("it shall be repaid") uses the same root as ΑΝΤΑΠΟΔΟΜΑ in verse 12, creating a deliberate contrast: human repayment is renounced in favor of divine repayment at the resurrection.

12. **14:15 — "Blessed is he who shall eat bread in the kingdom of God" (Makarios hostis phagetai arton en tē basileia tou Theou):** Sinaiticus reads ΜΑΚΑΡΙΟΣ ΟΣΤΙΣ ΦΑΓΕΤΑΙ ΑΡΤΟΝ ΕΝ ΤΗ ΒΑΣΙΛΕΙΑ ΤΟΥ ΘΕΟΥ. This exclamation by a fellow guest serves as the narrative bridge to the Great Supper parable. The beatitude expresses a common Jewish piety about the messianic banquet (cf. Isa 25:6-8). Jesus's response in the parable will dramatically redefine who actually participates in that banquet — not those who assume their invitation, but those who accept it.

13. **14:16-17 — "a great supper" (deipnon mega):** Sinaiticus reads ΔΕΙΠΝΟΝ ΜΕΓΑ. The ΔΕΙΠΝΟΝ was the main meal of the day, taken in the evening. The double invitation — first the initial invitation (ΕΚΑΛΕΣΕΝ ΠΟΛΛΟΥΣ, "he invited many"), then the summons at the hour of the meal (ΑΠΕΣΤΕΙΛΕΝ ΤΟΝ ΔΟΥΛΟΝ ΑΥΤΟΥ, "he sent his servant") — reflects actual ancient practice. The single servant (ΔΟΥΛΟΝ, singular) contrasts with Matthew 22:3-4 where multiple servants are sent, maintaining the parable's simpler Lukan form.

14. **14:18 — "with one accord" (apo mias):** Sinaiticus reads ΑΠΟ ΜΙΑΣ, an elliptical expression (literally "from one" — supplying ΓΝΩΜΗΣ, "mind" or ΦΩΝΗΣ, "voice"). The unanimity of refusal is emphatic. The three excuses follow a pattern of escalating irrevocability: a field can be inspected later, oxen can be tested later, but a marriage is a permanent change of status. Each excuse uses ΕΡΩΤΩ ΣΕ ΕΧΕ ΜΕ ΠΑΡΗΤΗΜΕΝΟΝ ("I ask you, have me excused"), a polite formula of declining that masks the insult of rejecting a feast already prepared.

15. **14:20 — "I have married a wife" (gynaika egēma):** Sinaiticus reads ΓΥΝΑΙΚΑ ΕΓΗΜΑ. This third excuse alone omits the polite formula "I ask you, have me excused" — the refusal is blunt and unapologetic. The appeal may allude to Deuteronomy 24:5, which exempts a newly married man from military service and public duties for one year. However, Jesus's parable subverts this exemption: no obligation, however legitimate, supersedes the summons to the kingdom feast.

16. **14:21 — "the master of the house, being angry" (orgistheis ho oikodespotēs):** Sinaiticus reads ΟΡΓΙΣΘΕΙΣ Ο ΟΙΚΟΔΕΣΠΟΤΗΣ. The anger (ΟΡΓΗ) of the master is the turning point of the parable. The command to go into ΠΛΑΤΕΙΑΣ ΚΑΙ ΡΥΜΑΣ ("streets and lanes") of the city — the broad avenues and narrow alleys — encompasses every quarter. The fourfold list of the invited (poor, maimed, blind, lame) deliberately echoes verse 13, confirming that the parable enacts the very instruction Jesus had given to his host.

17. **14:23 — "roads and hedges" (tas hodous kai phragmous):** Sinaiticus reads ΤΑΣ ΟΔΟΥΣ ΚΑΙ ΦΡΑΓΜΟΥΣ, the open roads and hedgerows outside the city. This second sending represents an expansion beyond the city walls — allegorically, beyond Israel to the Gentiles. The verb ΑΝΑΓΚΑΣΟΝ ("compel") does not imply violence but the urgent, insistent persuasion needed to convince social outcasts and strangers that they are genuinely welcome at a great man's table. The purpose clause ΙΝΑ ΓΕΜΙΣΘΗ ΜΟΥ Ο ΟΙΚΟΣ ("so that my house may be filled") expresses the master's determination that no place shall remain empty.

18. **14:24 — "none of those men who were invited" (oudeis tōn andrōn ekeinōn tōn keklēmenōn):** Sinaiticus reads ΟΥΔΕΙΣ ΤΩΝ ΑΝΔΡΩΝ ΕΚΕΙΝΩΝ ΤΩΝ ΚΕΚΛΗΜΕΝΩΝ. The shift to ΛΕΓΩ ΓΑΡ ΥΜΙΝ ("for I say to you," plural) is ambiguous: is the master of the house still speaking to his servant, or is Jesus now speaking directly to the dinner guests? The formal translation preserves this ambiguity by leaving the quotation open. The perfect participle ΚΕΚΛΗΜΕΝΩΝ ("who have been invited") emphasizes the standing invitation that was spurned.

19. **14:26 — "hate" (misei):** Sinaiticus reads ΜΙΣΕΙ. The Semitic usage of ΜΙΣΕΩ ("to hate") in this context means "to love less, to subordinate in loyalty" (cf. Gen 29:31 LXX, where Leah is "hated" meaning "less loved"; Mal 1:3; Rom 9:13). The formal translation retains "hate" as the direct equivalent of the Greek, preserving the deliberately shocking force of Jesus's demand. The list of seven relationships — father, mother, wife, children, brothers, sisters, and one's own life (ΨΥΧΗ) — is comprehensive and totalizing.

20. **14:27 — "bear his own cross" (bastazei ton stauron heautou):** Sinaiticus reads ΒΑΣΤΑΖΕΙ ΤΟΝ ΣΤΑΥΡΟΝ ΕΑΥΤΟΥ. The verb ΒΑΣΤΑΖΩ ("to bear, to carry") differs from the Synoptic parallels which use ΑΙΡΩ ("to take up," Mark 8:34; Matt 16:24). ΒΑΣΤΑΖΩ emphasizes the ongoing burden of carrying — not merely the initial act of taking up. The condemned criminal carrying his crossbeam (ΣΤΑΥΡΟΣ, patibulum) to the place of execution was a familiar sight in Roman Palestine. This is the third occurrence of "is not able to be my disciple" (ΟΥ ΔΥΝΑΤΑΙ ΕΙΝΑΙ ΜΟΥ ΜΑΘΗΤΗΣ), forming a threefold refrain with verses 26 and 33.

21. **14:28 — "count the cost" (psēphizei tēn dapanēn):** Sinaiticus reads ΨΗΦΙΖΕΙ ΤΗΝ ΔΑΠΑΝΗΝ. The verb ΨΗΦΙΖΩ (from ΨΗΦΟΣ, "pebble, counting-stone") means "to calculate, to reckon up" — the image is of counting stones used as an abacus. The noun ΔΑΠΑΝΗ ("cost, expense") occurs only here in the New Testament. The tower-building parable and the king-going-to-war parable (vv. 31-32) are twin illustrations of the same principle: discipleship requires sober assessment of what it will cost to follow through.

22. **14:31-32 — The parable of the warring king:** Sinaiticus reads ΒΟΥΛΕΥΣΕΤΑΙ ("will take counsel, will deliberate"), from ΒΟΥΛΕΥΩ, a term of political and military deliberation. The numbers — ten thousand against twenty thousand — present impossible odds, a ratio of one to two. The king who cannot win must SUE FOR PEACE (ΕΡΩΤΑ ΤΑ ΠΡΟΣ ΕΙΡΗΝΗΝ, literally "asks the things pertaining to peace"). The parable does not counsel surrender to an enemy but rather illustrates the folly of undertaking discipleship without counting the full cost of allegiance.

23. **14:33 — "renounce" (apotassetai):** Sinaiticus reads ΑΠΟΤΑΣΣΕΤΑΙ, a middle voice verb meaning "to take leave of, to renounce, to say farewell to." The same verb appears at Luke 9:61 ("let me first say farewell to those at my home") and Acts 18:18. Here the object is ΠΑΣΙΝ ΤΟΙΣ ΕΑΥΤΟΥ ΥΠΑΡΧΟΥΣΙΝ ("all his own possessions"), making the renunciation total. This is the conclusion that the two parables prepare: discipleship demands the surrender of everything, not merely what is convenient.

24. **14:34 — "salt" (to halas):** Sinaiticus reads ΤΟ ΑΛΑΣ. The salt saying appears in all three Synoptic Gospels (Mark 9:50; Matt 5:13) but in different contexts. Luke places it as the climax of the discipleship discourse, suggesting that salt represents the disciple's capacity for total commitment. The verb ΜΩΡΑΝΘΗ ("becomes tasteless," from ΜΩΡΑΙΝΩ, literally "to become foolish") plays on the double meaning: salt that has lost its savor is "foolish" salt — paralleling the disciple who undertakes the journey without counting the cost.

25. **14:35 — "neither for the earth nor for the dunghill" (oute eis gēn oute eis koprian):** Sinaiticus reads ΟΥΤΕ ΕΙΣ ΓΗΝ ΟΥΤΕ ΕΙΣ ΚΟΠΡΙΑΝ. Luke alone mentions the ΚΟΠΡΙΑ ("dunghill, manure heap"), indicating that tasteless salt is useless even as fertilizer — a detail absent from Matthew and Mark. The final exhortation Ο ΕΧΩΝ ΩΤΑ ΑΚΟΥΕΙΝ ΑΚΟΥΕΤΩ ("he who has ears to hear, let him hear") is a solemn formula marking the preceding teaching as requiring spiritual discernment (cf. 8:8). The imperative ΑΚΟΥΕΤΩ ("let him hear") closes the chapter with a direct challenge to the audience.

### Translation Philosophy

1. **Formal equivalence in Sabbath discourse:** Luke 14 opens with the third and final Sabbath healing controversy in the Gospel (cf. 6:6-11; 13:10-17). The formal translation preserves the structural parallels with chapter 13: the same argumentative pattern (question, silence/objection, a fortiori reasoning) and the same verbal links (ΛΥΕΙ/loosing in 13:15 parallels the implied release ΑΠΕΛΥΣΕΝ in 14:4). The compressed healing narrative of verse 4 — three verbs in rapid succession — is rendered without expansion.

2. **Banquet theology:** The entire chapter is set at a banquet, creating a sustained metaphor: literal dining customs (vv. 7-11), radical hospitality (vv. 12-14), and the eschatological feast (vv. 15-24) are woven together. The formal translation maintains the Greek vocabulary of reclining (ΚΑΤΑΚΛΙΘΗΝΑΙ, ΑΝΑΠΕΣΕ, ΑΝΑΚΛΙΘΗΝΑΙ, ΣΥΝΑΝΑΚΕΙΜΕΝΩΝ) rather than modernizing to "sit," preserving the cultural specificity of ancient dining practice.

3. **Cost of discipleship:** The three "is not able to be my disciple" sayings (vv. 26, 27, 33) form a deliberate triadic structure. The formal translation preserves the identical Greek phrasing (ΟΥ ΔΥΝΑΤΑΙ ΕΙΝΑΙ ΜΟΥ ΜΑΘΗΤΗΣ) in each instance, resisting the temptation to vary the English for stylistic purposes. The structural repetition is the point — each iteration raises the demand.

4. **Sinaiticus priority:** The reading ΥΙΟΣ ("son") at 14:5, where many manuscripts read ΟΝΟΣ ("donkey"), is a notable Sinaiticus variant that significantly strengthens the argument. The formal translation follows the Sinaiticus text without emendation. Other Sinaiticus readings are preserved throughout, including the specific wording of the excuses in verses 18-20 and the salt saying in verses 34-35.

5. **Structural coherence:** Luke 14 divides into five interconnected sections: the Sabbath healing (vv. 1-6), the parable of places at table (vv. 7-11), the instruction on radical hospitality (vv. 12-14), the Great Supper parable (vv. 15-24), and the cost of discipleship with twin parables and the salt saying (vv. 25-35). The banquet setting unifies the first four sections, while the fifth transitions to the open road (v. 25, "great crowds were going with him"), shifting from indoor instruction to public proclamation. The formal translation preserves the connecting particles (ΔΕ, ΟΥΝ, ΓΑΡ) that signal these transitions.
